posts.name: Paul Wi11enberg
posts.cooked:
I’m fascinated why many “natural wines” have become “cult wines.” I’ve got some theories which I’ll lay out below but I’m also interested in what your thoughts are. For reference, I’m talking about wines like:

Emmanuel Houillon (Maison Pierre Overnoy) Chardonnay Arbois Pupillin
Domaine de l’Anglore Tavel
Clos Rougeard (Foucault) Saumur-Champigny Les Poyeux

I think there’s a few things going on here:

  1. Limited Production: that’s a simple supply issue, but why the demand? two possible ideas:

  2. The Napa Cabs Appeal: For nouveau riche collectors that can’t wait 30 years for their Bordeaux to mature, they sought ready-to-drink wines instead. Natural wines can be earlier maturing due to lack of sulphur, oxidative winemaking techniques and other decisions traditional or not.

  3. Burgundian Appeal: The wines are, frankly, inconsistent. Which means the highs feel higher because the lows are lower. You might also think of this like the classic fairy tale plotline such as Cinderella. She’s got a crappy but decent life, she gets some hope by going to the ball, but then she loses that hope at midnight, only to attain infinite joy arguably because of the once lost but not infinite joy. I think that’s one appeal of great Burgundy, especially during it’s meteoric price rise of the 80s and 90s. The inconsistency of quality and utter disappointment made the highs feel higher. In conjunction with my theory of early maturing theory above, it’s a dice roll you’re opening the wine at peak, adding to the intrigue.

So am I off? Got a better theory for the cult status of many natural wines?

posts.name: Dennis Kanagie
posts.cooked: I think you’re only allowed to ride a bike or drive a hybrid to those wineries.

posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked: Rougeard inconsistent? Are you kidding?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: It should also be noted that the wines of Clos Rougeard are not ‘natural wines’ in the sense of being made without sulfur. They are utterly classic, artisanal wines. And they are also famously consistent in their high quality.

posts.name: Tvrtko Cernos
posts.cooked:
Rougeard a “cult natural wine” (AND inconsistent in the sense you specify)?
There has got to be some confusion either about the winery in question or about the terms of reference here.

posts.name: bob parsons alberta
posts.cooked:
Jamie Goode has just written this article on his blog. Just some reflections after a visit to SA.

http://www.wineanorak.com/wineblog/

link_counts.title: Jamie Goode's wine blog – blogging about wine since 2001

posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.cooked: this is an issue of producer and not of an individual wine of a producer as you listed. i dont think people only focus on silex of dagueneau as being his cult wine. all the producers you mention vinify in a way which may be considered natural but do not market themselves as natural. not to mention they all require just as much age to mature as ‘conventional’ wines. i am certain clos rougeard breze can outlive many top burgundies. if someone is making a quality product people will find out and there will be a demand. when there is small production there will eventually be over demand/under supply. hype is courtesy of the internet. it is pretty simple.

posts.name: patrick c albright
posts.cooked:
Nouveau rich?
Napa??
L’Anglore???

I am confused.

posts.name: Matt Latuchie
posts.cooked:
Keith Levenberg:

Rougeard inconsistent? Are you kidding?

Making a guess here, but I don’t think Paul is associating point #3 with the third producer (Clos Rougeard) that he listed. I believe he was saying that there’s a consistency problem in Burgundy, not with Clos Rougeard.

posts.cooked:
Matt Latuchie:
Keith Levenberg:

Rougeard inconsistent? Are you kidding?

Making a guess here, but I don’t think Paul is associating point #3 with the third producer (Clos Rougeard) that he listed. I believe he was saying that there’s a consistency problem in Burgundy, not with Clos Rougeard.

How inconsistent have you found the wines from top Burgundy producers? Rousseau? Truchot? d’Angerville? Etc.?

posts.name: Tvrtko Cernos
posts.cooked:
Matt Latuchie:

Making a guess here, but I don’t think Paul is associating point #3 with the third producer (Clos Rougeard) that he listed. I believe he was saying that there’s a consistency problem in Burgundy, not with Clos Rougeard.

So, just for the sake of argument, let’s assume he was actually trying to say what you are suggesting, “there’s a consistency problem in Burgundy”.

Or, on second thoughts, let’s not. Why add insult to injury?

posts.name: Jon Hesford
posts.cooked:
I’d never heard of Clos Rougeard until today when its iconic winemaker died. A number of wine writers that I respect posted about him so I would definitiely want to try his wines and those of his estate if they continue in the vein.

That said, I think there are a only a tiny few natural winemakers who are worth following. I think there are hundreds that are rubbish and fawned over by trendy retailers, sommeliers and bloggers. I’ve wasted more money on Natural wines than I have on any other category. But I’d rate Mas Olivier and Leon Barral and Michel Guignier as some of the best producers I know in their regions. Other Natural producers occasionally come out with some stunning wines amid lots of shit and some are clearly just along for the ride, taking the piss out of the numpties that pay big money to drink scrumpy.

There were about 3 or 4 Natural producers at the Northern Rhone foire in Ampuis last year and they were far and away the worst bags of shite on show.

However, I think there is a lot to be said for Natural in Moderation. This “hands-off” style is pretty much what every winemaker that I respect does.

Somewhere in between blandness and doing-f*ck-all is where it’s at. That applies to the restaurant, fashion and music world too.

posts.name: Tvrtko Cernos
posts.cooked:
bob parsons alberta:

Jamie Goode has just written this article on his blog. Just some reflections after a visit to SA.

Jamie Goode's wine blog – blogging about wine since 2001

That horse is so dead, if you ask me. What conclusions can be gleaned from the piece are conclusions that most wine lovers over 20 years of age at the time and with some experience of wine and life as most people know it would naturally have arrived at 10-15 years ago, when all this more-natural-than-thou nonsense first began to gather momentum. My impression is Jamie Goode is taking the long way around and, several books later, while displaying a level of studiousness than anyone must be commended for, whatever their endeavour , I think a lot of his reflections on “natural wine” tend to end up just stating the obvious, often in a slightly belaboured way.

posts.name: Dennis Kanagie
posts.cooked:
Tvrtko C.:

I think a lot of his reflections on “natural wine” tend to end up just stating the obvious, often in a slightly belaboured way.

Bet he never had trouble making the 500 word minimum for his essays in school.

posts.name: Tvrtko Cernos
posts.cooked:
Jon Hesford:

That said, I think there are a only a tiny few natural winemakers who are worth following. I think there are hundreds that are rubbish and fawned over by trendy retailers, sommeliers and bloggers. (…) Other Natural producers occasionally come out with some stunning wines amid lots of shit and some are clearly just along for the ride, taking the piss out of the numpties that pay big money to drink scrumpy.

Totally agree with this.

Which, when you think about it, kind of starts making “natural winemakers” sound a lot like everyone else, including not just other winemakers, but people in other walks of life as well: tinkers, tailors, soldiers, spies. No more than a tiny few are truly extraordinary, and, as for the rest, some are very good, some are decent, most are average, some are poor, and some are a total, utter disgrace to the human race .
Sounds like common sense to me.
The trouble starts when your main sales pitch is basically that “your shit don’t stink”.

posts.name: Tvrtko Cernos
posts.cooked:
Dennis Kanagie:

Bet he never had trouble making the 500 word minimum for his essays in school.

As a matter of fact, I heard most of his teachers eventually opted for early retirement.
Great post Jon.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Jon Hesford:

I’d never heard of Clos Rougeard until today when its iconic winemaker died. A number of wine writers that I respect posted about him so I would definitiely want to try his wines and those of his estate if they continue in the vein.

That said, I think there are a only a tiny few natural winemakers who are worth following.

This is a non sequitur, because Clos Rougeard does not produce ‘natural’ wine.
